fix(mcp): un-gate WaSendTool constructor from #[cfg(test)] (ft-ljgyr)

rustc E0599 blocker. WaSendTool::new at mcp_tools.rs:2000 and
WaSendTool::new_with_shared_rate_limiter at :2010 are both non-test
(`pub(super) fn`), and both delegate to
Self::with_wezterm_handle_and_shared_rate_limiter. That helper was
gated `#[cfg(test)]` — likely a slip in pane 2's ft-eu0no
shared-rate-limiter refactor. Under any non-test build the helper is
cfg-eliminated and the non-test callers see it as undefined.

Fix: remove the `#[cfg(test)]` on with_wezterm_handle_and_shared_rate_limiter.
The body is a plain struct ctor with no test-only deps, so it is safe
to expose in all builds. Its sibling `with_wezterm_handle` (also
`#[cfg(test)]`, at :2024) stays test-only — its only caller is the
:7077 test harness — so that gate is correct.

Ride-along (ft-eu0no, pane 2, uncommitted in working tree): this commit
also carries pane 2's in-flight build_policy_engine_with_shared_rate_limiter
threading through WaGetTextTool / WaSearchTool / WaSendTool /
WaWorkflowRunTool, plus what looks like an editor-glitch cluster of
six duplicated `let policy_rate_limiter = Arc::clone(&self.policy_rate_limiter);`
lines around :1535 that pane 2 should collapse in their next commit.
Flagging rather than cleaning up to respect pane-2 ownership.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-3l2p1","title":"[security][P3] web server has no auth middleware; allow_public_bind exposes /events + /search + /panes + /stream/* without credentials","description":"## Finding

crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/router.rs build_app wires three middlewares — BodySizeGuard, RequestSpanLogger, StateInjector — and NO authentication middleware. The router exposes /health, /panes, /events, /search, /bookmarks, /ruleset-profile, /saved-searches, /stream/events, /stream/deltas as unauthenticated GETs.

web/server.rs:18 + :67 gate binding on non-localhost behind Config.allow_public_bind, and log a 'remotely reachable' warning. But once the operator flips that opt-in (to expose the SSE feeds to a dashboard on another host, for example), every captured pane byte — which may include agent prompts containing API keys, session tokens, PII, or secrets not yet caught by the Redactor — is served to any network client.

/search and /events can exfiltrate entire captured output history via FTS5 queries; /stream/deltas is a live firehose.

## Recommended fix

Either:
(a) Forbid allow_public_bind entirely until bearer-token auth middleware lands.
(b) Add a ShareToken middleware gated on Config.require_auth (default=true when allow_public_bind=true), checking an HMAC'd bearer token against a rotating secret.
(c) Require TLS + client-cert auth when bind is non-localhost.

(a) is smallest diff and most defensive for an early-stage project.

## Acceptance Criteria
- allow_public_bind=true without an auth config fails at startup, not at request time.
- Tests demonstrate localhost binds still work with no auth (no regression for the default case).

## Severity rationale
P3 because allow_public_bind is already an explicit opt-in and the 'remotely reachable' warning logs at startup. Still a real-world footgun: an operator chasing 'why won't my dashboard work?' is likely to flip the bind without realising there is zero auth. Not P0/P1 because default config is safe.

 {"id":"ft-3l5bu","title":"[MEDIUM] RateLimiter.pane_counts HashMap leaks entries for every (pane_id, action) pair that is ever checked","description":"# Defect

## File + line

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2103` — `RateLimiter::check`.

## Concrete failure path

Every `check(action, Some(pane_id))` call executes

    let timestamps = self.pane_counts.entry((pane_id, action)).or_default();

which **always** inserts a `Vec<Instant>` under `(pane_id, action)` if it doesn't exist, before any limit logic runs. Same story for `global_counts` keyed by `ActionKind`.

After a pane is destroyed, nothing removes its entries. `prune_old(timestamps, window_start)` removes expired timestamps from the Vec but leaves the Vec itself and the HashMap entry in place. Over the lifetime of a long-running `PolicyEngine`, the HashMap grows monotonically:

* Bound per pane: at most one entry per `ActionKind` variant (finite enum).
* Bound across panes: `unique(pane_id) × |ActionKind|`.

On a 200+-pane swarm churning through ~1000 panes/day over 90 days, that's ~90k × |ActionKind| entries at ~16 bytes key + baseline Vec — not a crash, but a monotonic leak tracked nowhere. **Same class as ft-l6v1r** (`pane_activity_tracker`) and the `BackpressureMetrics::dropped_by_pane` leak I fixed earlier (f2390da9).

## Worse-case path: pane_id reuse

The ft-l6v1r commit observed that wezterm can reuse pane_ids. If a reused pane_id hashes to an old (pane_id, action) entry whose Vec still contains stale timestamps, the new pane inherits the dead pane's rate-limit budget — either under-counted (if the prune pass has already emptied the Vec) or over-counted (if timestamps haven't aged out yet). The current `check` path prunes based on `window_start` which is relative to `Instant::now()`, so stale timestamps from a destroyed pane will age out correctly — but entries without any timestamps will still mis-attribute the **first** rate-limited hit back to the old pane's history in any diagnostic that reads `pane_counts` directly.

## Fix scope

1. `RateLimiter::cleanup_pane(pane_id) -> usize` — remove every `(pane_id, _)` entry from `pane_counts`, return the number removed for telemetry / debug_assert. Cheap: one full iteration.
2. Unit test pinning: entries present before, zero after, unrelated panes untouched, idempotent on panes never inserted.
3. Wiring into the PaneDestroyed teardown path is a separate follow-up bead — `PolicyEngine` is not currently plumbed through `ObservationRuntime::spawn_discovery_task` or `handle_native_event`, and that plumbing is a broader cross-boundary change that belongs on its own bead.

Single-file scope: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs`.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.564595Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:54:31.244870Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:54:31.244127Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Pane 3 ft-yj375 (wa.state + robot state auth)

Touches:

- crates/frankenterm-core/tests/wa_state_mcp_conformance.rs — regression test

## Pane 4 TOON conformance + ft-2zbie regen

Touches:
- crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_mission_tx.rs — canonicalize + harness updates
- crates/frankenterm-core/tests/wa_mission_toon_mcp_conformance.rs (NEW) — TOON parallel harness
- 9 golden_robot_envelope/wa_mission_*.json + wa_tx_*.json — regenerated from placeholders

- **mcp_tools.rs** — ONLY pane 2 touching it. No direct conflict with pane 3 or 4.
- **main.rs** — ONLY pane 3 touching it. No direct conflict with pane 2.
- **policy.rs** — ONLY pane 2. Isolated.
- **storage.rs** — ONLY pane 2 (probably).
- **.beads/issues.jsonl** — ALL three panes + this reviewer touch it. JSONL is line-appendable; merges usually clean but can produce duplicate entries or re-ordered lines.

## Real risk: API signature drift

If pane 2's ft-eu0no changes PolicyEngine::authorize's receiver from &mut self to &self (plausible if the rate limiter moves into Arc<Mutex>), pane 3's in-flight main.rs edits may call authorize with the OLD signature. Second-to-land rebase hits a compile break. Compile breaks are recoverable; the risk is the 'verification partial' pattern we've seen 5+ times this session — the second pane may commit without re-running tests post-rebase.

## Concrete coordination step for first-to-land

- Whoever lands first: in the commit message, state the new PolicyEngine::authorize signature explicitly (if changed) so the second pane can rebase mechanically.
- Whoever lands second: re-run the file-specific cargo check before committing. Not the full test suite — just cargo check --lib -p frankenterm-core. Catches any signature drift within ~30s.
- Whoever lands third: same rule.

## Why LOW, not MEDIUM

- No silent-correctness risk; conflicts are compile-time visible.
- The three panes' edits don't mathematically overlap on source lines.
- Filing primarily so a post-hoc audit ('why did three consecutive verification-partial commits land') has a pointer to the coordination window.

 {"id":"ft-45805","title":"[mor:ergonomics] rch PreToolUse hook SIGTERMs cargo; failure mode should be 'fallback', not 'die'","description":"# Observation

From working memory + user experience across recent sessions:

- rch PreToolUse hook (\`force_remote=true\` in
  ~/.config/rch/config.toml) intercepts every Bash call containing
  \`cargo\` and routes to remote workers.
- When remote workers are unhealthy, the subprocess receives SIGTERM
  (exit 143).
- Local fallback exists only via workarounds (Python fork+setsid,
  \`enabled=false\` override, \`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-<name>\`
  bypass).

At 3am: operator needs to reproduce a test failure. Runs
\`cargo test foo\`. Shell returns exit 143 with no diagnostic.
Operator has to know the workaround exists. New operators or less-
ranked agents don't know the workaround and waste 30 minutes
re-trying.

This isn't frankenterm code, but it is "the development
environment frankenterm runs inside," and it breaks
frankenterm-the-project's ergonomics during incident response.

# Question

Two levers worth exploring:

1. **Fallback-on-worker-failure in rch itself.** If the remote
   workers SIGTERM the subprocess, catch the signal, run locally,
   mark the worker unhealthy. Exit 143 becomes a last resort,
   not the default failure mode.

2. **Project-level \`just\` or \`make\` target** that wraps the
   known-good build recipe: \`CC=...clang CXX=...clang++
   CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-local-target cargo <args>\`. The 3am
   operator runs \`just test foo\` and it always works — no memory
   of which env vars to set.

Concrete ask: would a one-line \`just build\` / \`just test\`
target in the frankenterm root, with the fork-bypass recipe
already baked in, materially improve the 3am experience? It's
~15 min of work and saves 30 min per incident per operator.

# 3am principle

Every workaround that lives only in memory (mine, yours, the
next-agent's) is a booby-trap for the on-call. Encode the
workaround in a target; when it stops being necessary, the target
becomes a no-op one-liner.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:41.686932Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.070631Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.611695Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","mor","rch","tooling"]}

 {"id":"ft-lekgj","title":"[MEDIUM] HeadlessMuxServer::Heartbeat silently ACKs unknown peers, producing split-brain with pruned federation state","description":"# Defect

## File + line

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/headless_mux_server.rs:423` — `HeadlessMuxServer::handle_request`, `RemoteRequest::Heartbeat` arm.

## Concrete failure path

```rust
RemoteRequest::Heartbeat { from, pane_count } => {
    if let Some(peer) = self.peers.get_mut(&from.node_id) {
        peer.last_heartbeat_at = epoch_ms();
        peer.pane_count = pane_count;
        peer.status = PeerStatus::Connected;
    }
    RemoteResponse::HeartbeatAck
}
```

**If the heartbeat arrives from a node that is NOT in `self.peers`, the handler silently drops it and still returns `HeartbeatAck`.** This is reachable in two natural situations:

1. **Post-prune race.** Peer N missed enough heartbeats to be marked `Unreachable` by `check_peer_health()`, then removed by `prune_unreachable_peers()`. The peer is still running, its network partition heals, and it sends its next heartbeat. Server has no record of N, silently ACKs, peer thinks federation is healthy, server thinks it has fewer peers. Classic split-brain.
2. **Heartbeat before JoinFederation.** A misconfigured or restarted peer starts heartbeating before its `JoinFederation` roundtrip completes (race on a lossy network where JoinFederation gets reordered behind the first Heartbeat). Server silently ACKs. Peer sees success. Server keeps acting without the peer in its registry.

In both cases:
- `ListPeers` on the server returns a stale list (missing the real peer).
- `federated_pane_count` undercounts — pane_count from the unknown peer is thrown away.
- No telemetry, no warn log.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer (split-brain between peer's belief and server's registry), affecting federation telemetry and potentially workload scheduling if `federated_pane_count` feeds a placement decision.
- Not a crash, not data corruption.
- Recovery requires the peer to notice it's not in ListPeers and re-`JoinFederation` — the peer has no prompt to do that today because HeartbeatAck looks successful.

## Fix sketch

Return a specific error when the peer is unknown so the sender can re-JoinFederation and recover automatically:

```rust
RemoteRequest::Heartbeat { from, pane_count } => {
    if let Some(peer) = self.peers.get_mut(&from.node_id) {
        peer.last_heartbeat_at = epoch_ms();
        peer.pane_count = pane_count;
        peer.status = PeerStatus::Connected;
        RemoteResponse::HeartbeatAck
    } else {
        RemoteResponse::Error {
            code: "peer_not_federated".into(),
            message: format!("node {} has not joined this server; re-send JoinFederation", from.node_id),
        }
    }
}
```

Clients receiving `peer_not_federated` should re-send `JoinFederation`. The error code is stable so clients can pattern-match.

## Tests

* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_for_unknown_peer_returns_error` — Server never saw `JoinFederation`, Heartbeat returns `Error { code: peer_not_federated, .. }` instead of `HeartbeatAck`.
* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_after_prune_returns_error` — Peer joined, went unreachable, got pruned, then sent a fresh heartbeat; same error surface so the peer knows to rejoin.
* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_for_known_peer_still_acks` — Regression: the happy path still yields `HeartbeatAck` and updates last_heartbeat_at/pane_count/status.

## Scope

Single file (`headless_mux_server.rs`). No public-API break — `RemoteResponse::Error` already exists and is already used for other failure modes in the same match.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:38:51.321265Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:27.477333Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:27.477025Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-lnlg4","title":"[reality-gap] tui/ftui_stub.rs named _stub but fully implemented (8296 LOC, header: 'no placeholders remain')","description":"# Code reality

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_stub.rs`:
  - 8296 lines (non-trivial)
  - Header comment line 14: "All view bodies (Home, Panes, Search,
    Help, Events, Triage, History, Timeline) are backed by live
    state — no placeholders remain."
  - `crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/mod.rs:103` declares
    `mod ftui_stub;` and re-exports `App, AppConfig, View,
    ViewState, run_tui` from it.

So the module name says "stub" but the header and the usage prove
it's the production FrankenTUI backend.

# Why this is a reality-check gap

Every sweep for placeholders/unimplemented code in this crate (this
one included) flags `ftui_stub` as a candidate for further
investigation, then discovers it's a false positive. Reality-check
sweeps burn cycles on this; code-review agents lose focus on real
gaps.

The naming is a legacy from when the file genuinely was a stub. The
content has grown into the real backend. The file name didn't
follow.

# Recommended disposition

```bash
git mv crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_stub.rs \
       crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_backend.rs
```

…then update the one `mod ftui_stub` declaration and the two
`super::ftui_stub::` re-export lines. 15-second edit, preserves
git history via `git mv`.

# Risk

- `git mv` preserves blame + history.
- No external dependency on the file name (it's a sibling module).
- Existing grep-based tooling that searches for "ftui_stub" would
  miss it post-rename — worth a global grep + a deprecation comment
  left at `mod.rs` during the rename window.

# Severity

Lowest of the 5 reality-check gaps filed this pass (ft-z01cl,
ft-trby3, ft-6269x, ft-x4k2b are all higher-impact), but the
cheapest to fix.  {"id":"ft-lokam","title":"[LOW] diag bundle environment.json leaks absolute workspace cwd; tracing fields leak operator paths","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #6 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Three logging/telemetry surfaces all come out clean on the obvious
secret-leak vectors (no bearer-token / API-key / pane-content
fields in tracing calls; no /etc/ or .ssh/ reads in diag
collection). One defense-in-depth gap remains: path privacy.

## Findings

### F1 [LOW] diag bundle environment.json includes absolute cwd
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/diagnostic.rs:87-89
  ```rust
  cwd: std::env::current_dir()
      .ok()
      .map(|p| p.display().to_string()),
  ```
- Diag bundles (`ft diag bundle`) are often attached to bug reports
  and shared outside the host. An absolute cwd reveals:
  - the user's home path segment (`/Users/<username>/...` or
    `/home/<username>/...`),
  - the project path (`/Users/alice/projects/secret-startup-pitch-deck/`),
  - on some setups, mount points that hint at filesystem layout.
- Severity: LOW. Users implicitly share the workspace when
  debugging, but the current behavior removes the choice.

### F2 [LOW] tracing fields log operator-private paths
- Multiple `tracing::[info|warn|debug]!(..., path = %X.display(), ...)`
  call sites scatter socket paths, DB paths, and workspace paths
  through structured logs. Representative hits (non-exhaustive):
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs:258, 302, 331,
      362, 401 — unix socket paths.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:1118 —
      `info_span!("telemetry_store_open", path = %db_path.display())`.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs:1375, 2658, 2814 —
      socket paths on accept failures.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/build_coord.rs:265 —
      `meta_path = %self.meta_path.display()`.
- Severity: LOW. These logs land in the user's local log file,
  not network-visible by default, but they do end up in diag
  bundles when `collect_recent_log_lines` is called.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- Bearer tokens in structured logs: grep across
  `tracing::{info,debug,warn,error}!` call sites for
  `token|bearer|secret|password|api_key` returned zero production
  hits. Clean.
- Pane content in tracing fields: no production code logs
  `segment.content`, `delta_text`, or `pane_text`. Only test
  `assert_eq!(segment.content, ...)` references found. Clean.
- Diag bundle reading privileged files: no `/etc/`, `.ssh/`,
  `.aws/`, `.gnupg/` reads in `diagnostic.rs`. The bundle only
  reads the ft SQLite DB (already redacted via `redact_events`)
  and writes to the workspace-local `diag_dir`. Clean.
- Environment variables in diag bundle: `gather_environment`
  captures only OS / arch / rust_version / wa_version / cwd —
  no `std::env::vars()` dump. Clean.
- Summarize_config: the docstring already calls out "no paths or
  secrets" and the implementation confirms — only numeric/boolean
  config values are copied over. Clean.

## Fix direction
- F1: replace absolute cwd with just the basename, or gate
  inclusion behind `DiagnosticOptions.include_cwd: bool` default
  `false`. Concretely at diagnostic.rs:87:
  ```rust
  cwd: if opts.include_cwd {
      std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|p| p.display().to_string())
  } else {
      std::env::current_dir()
          .ok()
          .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
  },
  ```
- F2: when a log line is included in a diag bundle via
  `collect_recent_log_lines` (or equivalent), run it through the
  existing `Redactor` path-aware rules. Non-bundle in-process logs
  stay as-is since they aid operator debugging on the local box.

## Severity rationale
- LOW (P2): neither finding reveals a secret, and both sit behind
  an operator action (`ft diag bundle` + sharing it). The fix
  shrinks the unintentional-share footprint but doesn't close a
  live exploit.

## Related
- ft-ybtyg (598a7c13) — snapshot/checkpoint hashing honesty.
- ft-2guux (doc reality-check) — complementary; this bead is the
  diagnostics-exfil counterpart.

 {"id":"ft-ltpog","title":"native_events: decode errors logged at debug, invisible in production","description":"# [MEDIUM] native_events: malformed wire events logged at debug

**Subsystem:** native_events / protocol_recovery
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs (`handle_connection_with_cx`)

## The hole

When wire-event decoding fails (invalid JSON, bad base64, unknown event
type), the handler emitted:

\`\`\`rust
Err(err) => {
    debug!(error = %err, "failed to decode native event (cx path)");
}
\`\`\`

A malformed wire event is a protocol anomaly — version skew between
frankenterm and the mux-server, on-wire corruption, or a hostile peer
writing into the native-events socket. Operators routinely filter
`debug!` in production, so the signal was effectively lost.

Contrast with the `MAX_EVENT_LINE_BYTES` path a few lines above, which
already emits at `warn!`. Same class of anomaly, inconsistent level.

## Fix

Promoted the decode-error log to `warn!`. The `warn!` import was already
present — no dependency change.

Future work (separate bead): attach a telemetry counter for
decode-failure rate so it can drive a SLI rather than relying solely on
log sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:39.950825Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568904Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568595Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["native-events","observability"]}

 {"id":"ft-yj375","title":"[security][P1] wa.state MCP tool + ft robot state CLI return pane title/cwd UNREDACTED (mismatched invariant with /panes)","description":"## Finding

Audit under ft-h8da2 (docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md) confirms: wa.state MCP tool and ft robot state CLI serve pane `title` and `cwd` **unredacted**, while the sibling web handler on `GET /panes` **does** redact them.

### Evidence

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:817-834 — McpPaneState::from_pane_info:

      title: info.title.clone(),
      cwd: info.cwd.clone(),

- crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:5501-5518 — PaneState::from_pane_info (CLI): same pattern, same unredacted clone.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/handlers.rs:64-70 — PaneView::from_record (web): **CORRECT** reference implementation:

      title: r.title.map(|t| redactor.redact(&t)),
      cwd: r.cwd.map(|c| redactor.redact(&c)),

### Threat

Pane titles are set by whatever process the pane hosts. Agents that bake credentials into the title (e.g. `codex --api-key sk-ant-...` visible in `argv[0]` → terminal title) or workflows that `printf '\\e]0;token=\\\\a'` will leak those values through wa.state and ft robot state.

A peer MCP agent that calls wa.state as part of a normal swarm-wide health check receives the raw title; a network-exposed ft robot state call over a subprocess bridge does the same. Redaction was applied to the web /panes endpoint for this exact reason; the MCP and CLI peers must match.

### Fix

Two equivalent options:

Option A (one-line per call-site, web-matching): in the wa.state and ft robot state handlers, after `McpPaneState::from_pane_info` / `PaneState::from_pane_info`, run the fields through a static `Redactor`:

    let redactor = Redactor::new();
    for s in &mut states {
        if let Some(t) = s.title.as_mut() { *t = redactor.redact(t); }
        if let Some(c) = s.cwd.as_mut()   { *c = redactor.redact(c); }
    }

Option B: change the from_pane_info signatures to accept `&Redactor` and redact inside (symmetrical with web/handlers.rs:64-70).

Option A is smaller-diff and doesn't ripple through test fixtures; preferred.

### Acceptance Criteria

- wa.state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- ft robot state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- Regression test: seed a pane with title containing `sk-ant-api03-XXXX`, invoke both surfaces, assert the raw token does not appear in the response JSON.
- docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md wa.state + ft robot state rows flip from `✗ LEAK` to `✓`.

### Why P1

Real leak on the default configuration — no operator opt-in required. Blast radius is narrow (titles/cwds, not pane scrollback) but automatic (fleet-wide state poll). Fix is mechanical.

Surfaced by RusticMeadow while landing ft-h8da2's audit matrix, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:28:41.743939Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:40:33.038204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:40:33.037749Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 4d26269f: wa.state and ft robot state redact title/cwd before serialization","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","cli","mcp","redaction","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-yjt9e","title":"policy::RateLimiter::pane_counts leaks entries forever — no eviction on pane destroy or GC path","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2076` — `RateLimiter.pane_counts: HashMap<(u64, ActionKind), Vec<Instant>>`. Every unique `(pane_id, action)` pair `check()` sees creates an entry. Nothing removes them.

A grep confirms it:

    $ rg 'pane_counts\.(remove|clear|retain)|fn gc|fn cleanup|fn prune_panes' crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs
    (no matches)

## Failure shape

On a long-running ft watcher with high pane churn (the 200+-pane fleet the project is designed for), every closed pane leaves a stale `(pane_id, action)` entry in `pane_counts` — one per ActionKind the pane ever touched. Over weeks those entries accumulate:

- Each entry holds a `Vec<Instant>` of up to `limit_per_pane` entries (default 60, configurable up to u32::MAX).
- After the sliding window expires, `prune_old` empties the Vec on the next `check()` — but only if check() is called with that (pane_id, action) again, which never happens for a destroyed pane.
- The entry itself is never removed, so the HashMap grows monotonically.

For 10k destroyed panes × 5 action kinds × 16B-per-Instant × 60-entries-peak = ~48 MB of leaked state. Not a crash, but a silent drift that scales with fleet uptime.

Global side (`global_counts: HashMap<ActionKind, Vec<Instant>>`) is BOUNDED by the finite ActionKind enum — not affected.

# Fix

Add two method shapes to `impl RateLimiter`:

1. `remove_pane(&mut self, pane_id: u64)` — iterate pane_counts, retain only keys whose first element ≠ pane_id. Called from the runtime's PaneDestroyed handler (same handler that already calls `remove_runtime_pane_state_for_pane` in runtime.rs:3574).

2. `gc(&mut self)` (or `gc_at(&mut self, now: Instant)` for testability) — iterate pane_counts, for each entry call prune_old with now-window as cutoff, drop the whole entry if the resulting Vec is empty. Safe opportunistic cleanup that catches panes closed without an explicit PaneDestroyed signal (crashes, abrupt detaches).

Also add operational telemetry: `pane_entries_evicted: u64` counter so operators can see the leak being managed.

# Tests

Inline `#[cfg(test)]` in policy.rs:

- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane`
- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_leaves_other_panes_intact`
- `rate_limiter_gc_drops_expired_empty_entries`
- `rate_limiter_gc_preserves_still-active_entries`

# Severity

MEDIUM — leak is slow, bounded per pane, doesn't panic. But it's cumulative and silent. Fleet operators would only see the symptom as gradual RSS creep over uptime-weeks, attributed to anything-but-rate-limiter. Shipping the eviction path closes the hole before someone has to bisect it from production.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.703056Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.104139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.103827Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of 27ef45e1 (absorbed commit includes ft-yjt9e methods: RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count + 4 regression tests: remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane, remove_pane_leaves_global_counts_intact, gc_drops_expired_empty_entries, gc_preserves_still_active_entries). Lib compiles clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Fix path: add one deny_policy_blocks_<tool>_mutation test per gated tool, asserting (a) envelope shape, (b) no downstream FS or mutation happened (e.g., mission file still at prior state), and (c) hint string for require_approval path.

Severity LOW — not a regression today. The gate IS working under default/allow policies (verified by existing tests continuing to pass). Filing so the next pane picking up ft-vue5t can close this in the same cargo invocation. Audit round 19, 2026-04-23.

 {"id":"ft-yskcu","title":"continuous_backpressure EwmaSmoother + sigmoid NaN poison cascade","description":"## Defect

Same NaN-poison pattern as ft-761tz (disk_pressure), in a sibling subsystem:
\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\`.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`ContinuousBackpressure::update(capture_ratio, write_ratio)\` where
   one ratio is \`NaN\`. Easy trigger: an external smoothed source that hit a div-by-zero
   guard upstream and quietly emitted NaN; or any test/consumer using \`observe_ratio\`
   directly.
2. Line 225-226: \`capture_ratio.clamp(0.0, 1.0)\` returns NaN — \`f64::clamp\` propagates
   NaN by design.
3. Line 228: \`self.capture_smoother.update(NaN)\`. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105)
   has **no NaN guard** — \`alpha.mul_add(NaN, ...)\` → NaN → \`self.value = NaN\`
   permanently. Every subsequent \`update()\` call preserves the NaN.
4. Line 231: \`smoothed_capture.max(smoothed_write)\` — \`f64::max\` does NOT propagate NaN
   under IEEE-754 (returns the non-NaN operand), but if both are NaN you get NaN.
5. Line 232-236: \`severity(NaN, _, _)\` → calls local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) which does
   \`x.clamp(-500.0, 500.0)\` — same NaN-propagation footgun. Returns NaN.
6. Line 232: \`self.current_severity = NaN\` permanently.
7. \`compute_actions\` (line 287-298) multiplies/mul_adds severity into
   \`poll_multiplier\`, \`pane_skip_fraction\`, \`detection_skip_fraction\`,
   \`buffer_limit_fraction\` — every field becomes NaN. ThrottleActions emitted to
   downstream throttle consumers are entirely poisoned.
8. \`BackpressureSnapshot\` (line 320) exposes \`severity\` and \`queue_ratio\` as
   \`f64\`. JSON serialization of NaN is invalid in strict JSON; \`serde_json\` emits
   \`null\` silently, corrupting telemetry payloads downstream.
9. Lucky accident: \`severity_to_tier(NaN)\` returns Black (NaN < x is always false,
   final else branch), so the discrete tier surface fails closed by IEEE-754
   coincidence rather than design. The continuous severity surface fails OPEN.

## Severity rationale

- Same class of silent wrong answer as ft-761tz.
- Continuous surface (severity, queue_ratio, ThrottleActions) fails OPEN — values
  become NaN, poll_multiplier becomes NaN, downstream throttle math becomes useless.
- Discrete \`equivalent_tier\` happens to fail closed by accident, not by design.
- Telemetry consumers (snapshot serialization) silently lose data.

## Fix sketch

1. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105) — if \`raw.is_nan()\`, return \`self.value\`
   without state change. Same defense as disk_pressure \`EwmaEstimator::update\`.
2. Local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) — short-circuit \`is_nan()\` to \`0.5\` like the
   backpressure_severity sibling does.
3. \`ContinuousBackpressure::update\` (line 224) — defense in depth: if either
   ratio is NaN, return \`current_actions()\` without state change.
4. Regression tests: pin each guard plus end-to-end NaN→Black-tier→clean recovery.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. No changes to \`backpressure.rs\` or \`backpressure_severity.rs\`
(both already hardened).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:05:30.189872Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:28.985819Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:28.985530Z","close_reason":"Landed in edab97f4 (fix(continuous_backpressure): NaN fail-closed across EmaSmoother, sigmoid, and update)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-yv02l","title":"[P2] Audit placeholder/stub markers surfaced by mock-code-finder sweep","description":"Surfaced by /mock-code-finder static scan of
crates/frankenterm-core/src/ and frankenterm/ (pane 5, session
2026-04-20). Seven findings; none reach ship-blocking severity, but
each warrants a targeted review to either remove the placeholder,
document the invariant that makes it safe, or replace with a real

Categories:

  2. unimplemented!() stubs
  3. placeholder values overwritten later

Findings:

  `collect_system_memory` returns `(0, 0)` on non-Linux/non-macOS.
  Why it matters: a future Windows/BSD target would silently receive
  zero memory readings instead of an error. Consider returning
